full_response:
We work with the ordinary Schwarzschild line–element

ds² = −(1−2GM/r) dt² + dr² /(1−2GM/r) + r² dΩ² ,

but now look at the formally-extended domain               r<0  
(the “fifth region’’ that would lie on the other side of the
curvature singularity at r=0).

1.  Radial null curves  
   Set dθ=dφ=0 and ds²=0.  Then

   0 = −(1−2GM/r) dt² + dr² /(1−2GM/r)           ⇒

   (dt/dr)² = 1 /(1−2GM/r)² = [ r /(r−2GM) ]² ,           (1)

   so that

   dt/dr = ± r /(r−2GM).                                  (2)

   In region r<0 the fraction r /(r−2GM) is positive
   (both numerator and denominator are negative).
   “Outgoing’’ means moving away from the singularity at r=0,
   i.e. toward more negative values of r ( dr<0 ).
   We therefore choose the sign that makes dt>0 when dr<0,

   dt/dr = − r /(r−2GM).                                  (3)

2.  Integrating t(r)  
   Start the light-ray at some arbitrary r0<0 and let it propagate
   outward to r<r0 (more negative).  Integrating eq. (3),

   t(r) − t0 = − ∫_{r0}^{r}  r' /(r'−2GM) dr'.            (4)

   The integrand can be split as

   r' /(r'−2GM) = 1 + 2GM /(r'−2GM),                     (5)

   so that

   t(r) − t0 = −[ r' + 2GM ln|r'−2GM| ]_{r0}^{r}
            =  (|r| − |r0|) − 2GM ln
              ( |r−2GM| / |r0−2GM| ).                     (6)

3.  Behaviour as the ray proceeds to infinity  
   Send the ray farther and farther outward, r → −∞ :

      |r|  → ∞  and  |r−2GM| ≈ |r|  so  ln|r−2GM| ∼ ln|r|.

   Hence

   t(r) ≈ |r|  +  constant  −  2GM ln|r| .                (7)

   The first term grows linearly with |r| and dominates the
   slowly-growing logarithm.  Therefore

          r  →  −∞   ⇒   t(r)  →  +∞ .                    (8)

4.  Result  
   No matter which negative radius r0 one starts from, an
   outgoing radial null geodesic can always continue to larger
   |r| (smaller r) and in doing so lets the Schwarzschild time
   coordinate t grow without bound.  Hence every such light-ray
   can reach arbitrarily late (indeed infinite) future time.

\boxed{\text{For every starting point r_0<0, an outgoing radial null
geodesic can be extended to r\to -\infty, along which the Schwarzschild
time coordinate increases without bound; i.e.\ it always reaches
t\to +\infty.}} 

Confidence: 71%
